claim: High cardiopulmonary fitness causes decreased mortality rate.

result.claim: High cardiopulmonary fitness causes decreased mortality rate.
result.reasoning:
The claim that high cardiopulmonary fitness causes decreased mortality rate is strongly supported by evidence from multiple human cohort studies.

Paper ID 09fd62d790996fac273fbbbb1449f19e885ced71 (Credibility Rating: 2.8/5, Cohort study, Sample Size: 833, Study Duration: 17.7 ± 11.8 years) found a significant inverse relationship between changes in cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) and all-cause, cardiovascular disease (CVD), and cancer mortality. Specifically, each 1 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ increase in CRF was associated with a 10.8%, 14.7%, and 15.9% reduction in all-cause, CVD, and cancer mortality, respectively.

Similarly, Paper ID W3003268927 (Credibility Rating: 2.3/5, Cohort study, Sample Size: 2905, Study Duration: 19.0 ± 11.7 years) reported that cardiorespiratory fitness normalized to fat-free mass (V˙O2peakFFM) was inversely related to all-cause, cardiovascular disease, and cancer mortality. A 1 mL·kg FFM⁻¹·min⁻¹ improvement in V˙O2peakFFM was associated with a 16.2%, 8.4%, and 8.0% lower risk, respectively. This study also indicated that V˙O2peakFFM was a significantly stronger predictor of all-cause mortality than V˙O2peak normalized to total body weight.

While Paper ID 0281d08a1906c71269bfce359521fdc0eeac8b9d (Credibility Rating: 2.5/5, Cohort study, Sample Size: 48) found no association between severely low CRF and all-cause mortality in patients receiving hemodialysis, this finding is specific to a particular patient population and the authors themselves noted that larger cohort studies are needed to establish strong conclusions. Therefore, this does not significantly refute the general claim. Paper ID 39514761 (Credibility Rating: 3.0/5, Animal study) also provided supportive evidence, but its applicability to humans is limited due to being an animal study.

The consistent findings from two substantial human cohort studies provide strong evidence to support the claim.

**Evidence Note:** 09fd62d790996fac273fbbbb1449f19e885ced71, W3003268927, 0281d08a1906c71269bfce359521fdc0eeac8b9d, 39514761

evidence.text: Physical fitness is a combination of performance in the muscular and cardiopulmonary systems.
evidence.source: Associations Between Physical Activity, Fitness, Perceived Health, Chronic Disease and Mortality in Adult Survivors of Childhood and Young Adult Hodgkin's Lymphoma: A Scoping Review.

evidence.text: Loss of muscle mass and strength is associated with increased mortality.

evidence.text: The research examined the association of the long-term change in cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) with all-cause and disease-specific mortality.

evidence.text: Cardiorespiratory fitness was evaluated through the cardiopulmonary exercise test.

evidence.text: Depressive symptoms were identified as being associated with cardiopulmonary fitness.

evidence.text: Higher exercise capacity inversely correlates with early mortality rates.

evidence.text: Adult survivors of Hodgkin Lymphoma (HL) exhibit deficits in cardiopulmonary fitness.

evidence.text: Rizwan et al. reported that 50% (32/64) of adult survivors of HL evaluated for cardiopulmonary fitness via cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) had poor cardiopulmonary fitness (relative peak VO2 peak < predicted 85%).
evidence.source: Associations Between Physical Activity, Fitness, Perceived Health, Chronic Disease and Mortality in Adult Survivors of Childhood and Young Adult Hodgkin's Lymphoma: A Scoping Review.
